RT @Threatlabz: Zscaler ThreatLabz identified a SmartApeSG-linked supply chain attack that targeted the Okendo Reviews widget impacting thousands of e-commerce websites. The attack injected malicious JavaScript in all downstream websites that use the review widget. This activity is consistent with broader SmartApeSG infection chains that include ClickFix-style social engineering.

Read the full analysis here: https://t.co/beup3CDscS
items.author: virusbtn

items.content:
RT @JamfThreatLabs: A new macOS Nova Stealer campaign is riding the AI hype. 📷

Jamf Threat Labs is tracking a GitHub account pushing fake "free Claude" and "ChatGPT trial" repos. Each README walks the victim through pasting a curl | bash one-liner into Terminal — a ClickFix-style spin on the classic drag-to-Terminal trick, dressed up as free AI access.

The payload is an unsigned universal Mach-O. The install command runs xattr -c to strip the quarantine flag and sidestep Gatekeeper before execution. Once running, it behaves like the rest of the Nova Stealer family — modular, crypto-wallet-focused, harvesting sensitive user data.

The novelty isn't the malware — it's wrapping a well-worn macOS technique in an "AI for free" disguise. 
IoCs below 📷

#macOS #infostealer #NovaStealer #threatintel

items.content:
A PowerShell sample (d7d17646d5ed29c9299be936f034f902) recently uploaded to VirusTotal appears highly likely to be related to the APT38-attributed activity seen in the Axios NPM compromise, although it introduces several notable changes:

▶️Shared characteristics:
- Same persistence mechanism
- Same User-Agent string
- Same victim UID generation logic

▶️Notable differences:
- C2/URL delivery now relies on an {ARG} placeholder
- C2 communication use raw JSON in the POST body instead of Base64-encoded data
- Expanded reconnaissance, including installed applications, browser extensions, AV products, and administrator privilege checks
- Heavy capabilities such as PE injection and file browsing have been removed

The overall codebase still shares enough similarities to suggest lineage, while indicating the operator is actively evolving the malware.

items.content:
RT @DailyDarkWeb: ⚠️ Alleged Windows Kernel Exploit Framework Leaked by Orcinus Orca

A threat actor linked to Orcinus Orca has published what they claim is the full source code of "OrcaHunter Kernel Framework v1.0" alongside a purported pre-auth remote RCE/DoS proof-of-concept targeting Windows 11 24H2.

According to the post, the leak allegedly includes:

* Full OrcaHunter Framework v1.0 source code
* 1,300+ lines of claimed exploit framework code
* A pre-auth remote packet exploit targeting tcpip.sys
* Windows 11 24H2 build 26100.8655 and below listed as targets
* Core driver-related components
* Automation modules for reverse engineering and kernel vulnerability research

The actor claims the framework is designed to support low-level kernel research, dynamic emulation, constraint solving, and automated analysis of Windows .sys binaries.

If authentic, the claimed capability could be highly significant due to the alleged targeting of the Windows networking stack. Pre-authentication vulnerabilities in network-facing components can create serious enterprise risk if weaponized.

Potential impact if validated:

* Remote code execution
* Denial-of-service conditions
* Kernel-level compromise
* Pre-authentication attack surface exposure
* Rapid weaponization by ransomware and intrusion groups
* Increased targeting of Windows 11 enterprise environments

At this stage, the claim remains unverified. The existence of source code does not confirm a working zero-day or reliable exploit chain. Technical validation is required to determine whether the release contains a real vulnerability, a crash-only PoC, incomplete research code, or fabricated material.

Analyst Note: Exploit claims involving tcpip.sys and pre-auth remote execution should be treated as high-priority intelligence leads, but not as confirmed exploitation until independently reproduced. The most important next step is controlled technical validation in an isolated lab environment.

#DDW #Intelligence #DarkWeb #Windows

items.content:
Researcher found a design flaw in EasyAntiCheat's driver that allows injecting and executing unsigned code inside any EAC-protected game process. Even with HVCI and Secure Boot enabled.

Covers how EAC bridges between the kernel driver and the game's modules and how a flaw in that bridge gives unrestricted code execution.

Post is from 2021 but the design patterns in anti-cheat service initialization remain relevant.

Blog: https://t.co/IQpjy8OX4z
Video: https://t.co/1lLzzCx7z2

Authors: @sinclairq8 @_xeroxz 

#AntiCheat #ReverseEngineering #InfoSec
items.author: cr3ghost

items.content:
RT @IntCyberDigest: 🚨‼️ BREAKING: The threat actor who breached Novo Nordisk, the company behind Ozempic, has leaked 264 GB of data. They are also mocking Novo Nordisk, claiming the company was using passwords like "novo123" for critical systems and that its security team sucks.

The breach includes source code, proprietary information on both marketed and pipeline drugs, clinical trial records, data on employees, doctors, and patients, and manufacturing details. The threat actor also claims it obtained private internal AI models from Novo's systems.

They allegedly breached Novo in March via a GitHub access token that let it clone the company's repositories and find additional credentials. The intrusion ran for over two months and yielded roughly 1.3 terabytes across more than 700,000 files.

It’s the clearest sign yet that talks are moving forward and it reflects an understanding that no AI model can be completely immune to hacking.

items.author: FuzzySec

items.content:
RT @FBICyberDiv: Today, as part of Operation Endgame, the FBI joins our international law enforcement partners in announcing the disruption of SocGholish malware. SocGholish, active since 2018, is a Java-script based malware that masquerades as a legitimate browser update via compromised websites. The malware establishes an initial foothold into victim computers, collectively known as a botnet, and is then used by threat actors for further targeting with ransomware campaigns and espionage. As part of the operation, 106 servers and domains were taken down, 14,971 websites were remediated, the botnet was disabled, and victims were notified.

This action is part of Operation Riptide, an ongoing FBI campaign targeting the criminal actors, infrastructure, and financial networks behind cybercrime, cyber-enabled crime, and fraud against the American people.

Learn more: https://t.co/ob4ugpxmDG

items.author: NinjaParanoid

items.content:
RT @GrapheneOS: We built an initial release of GrapheneOS based on Android 17 (2026061700) but aren't going to release it through our Alpha channel due to discovering a serious upstream bug. Android 17 broke support for sideloading updates via recovery unless the OS images are large enough to exhaust COW space.

The stock Pixel OS is drastically larger than GrapheneOS due to having a massive amount of additional bundled app code for Google Mobile Services, many other Google apps and various Pixel apps. It's always above the threshold triggering the fallback code path for sideloading OS updates in recovery.

Over-the-air updates from both older versions to Android 17 and Android 17 to Android 17 work fine. It's only sideloading impacted by this. We don't want to release an OS version with broken OS update sideloading so we've cancelled 2026061700 and are building 2026061800 with a workaround for it.

Our current workaround is to force enable the fallback code path triggered by large OS images. This will fix sideloading an Android 17 version of GrapheneOS to another Android 17 version of GrapheneOS. However, sideloading Android 17 updates to older versions won't work without a further workaround.

We've tried making a build with a randomly generated 1GiB file included to make GrapheneOS about as large as the stock Pixel OS which fully works around the issue. We're not actually going to do that but rather we'll use the workaround forcing the fallback path for now and we'll find a proper fix.

Our workaround will provide working sideloading from our initial Android 17 release to a future release. However, it isn't currently possible to sideload from 16 QPR2. We could make an extra 16 QPR2 update for people who only sideload updates with the workaround to use until we make a proper fix.

Google didn't run into this because they add so much bloat to the OS for Google Mobile Services including Google Play services along with a bunch of other Google and Pixel apps. Pixel OS is a lot smaller than the OS on most Android devices but it's drastically larger than AOSP and even GrapheneOS.

GrapheneOS uses ahead-of-time compilation for Java/Kotlin code which greatly increases the size of the apps in the OS images. Despite this, it's still drastically smaller than the Pixel OS. It would be substantially larger if we bundled as much code as they do but instead it's the opposite...

items.author: d4rksystem

items.content:
Fed an obfuscated game cheats module into my personal AI RE framework to see if it could pinpoint specific functionalities. already fully reversed it myself, so I knew the answers.

DeepSeek V4 Pro: stopped it after nearly an hour of endless spinning.
GLM 5.2: took 30 mins. 25% incomplete or flat-out wrong.
GPT 5.5: finished in 20 mins. Better, but still 15% missed/incorrect.
Opus 4.8: instantly flagged for CVP violation lmao.

Hear Fable 5 might make a comeback soon, but pretty sure it’s not for me.😇
items.author: kernullist

items.content:
RT @FBICyberDiv: Today, as part of Operation Endgame, the FBI joins our international law enforcement partners in announcing the disruption of SocGholish malware. SocGholish, active since 2018, is a Java-script based malware that masquerades as a legitimate browser update via compromised websites. The malware establishes an initial foothold into victim computers, collectively known as a botnet, and is then used by threat actors for further targeting with ransomware campaigns and espionage. As part of the operation, 106 servers and domains were taken down, 14,971 websites were remediated, the botnet was disabled, and victims were notified.

This action is part of Operation Riptide, an ongoing FBI campaign targeting the criminal actors, infrastructure, and financial networks behind cybercrime, cyber-enabled crime, and fraud against the American people.

Learn more: https://t.co/ob4ugpxmDG

items.author: MarcOverIP

items.content:
Well, they actually do. Here is the BUT: As the authorities know that under the current technical limitations a reliable identification of VPN traffic from citizens and banning it completely is not doable, they resorted to a more complex solution. 

1. Have official VPNs: VPN usage without a permit is generally a problem, operating one even more. But cracking down on illegal VPN operators ( with endpoints outside of China ) is tricky and means you have track payments, identify operators ( sellers ) personally etc. That is a lot of work. 
They do that at times and people get fined or even more severely punished. But so far that has not stopped CN vpn operations. Running official VPNs is a good option to keep things funneled. Of course, it is a little bit contra productive to your censorship approaches, but as long as it is limited to a small group of your own citizens or foreigners, that is ok. Further, you can compel the operators with letting you listen into the traffic ( the fact that they shut down their operations during "sensitive times" is a good indicator they are close to the authorities ). So you know, what your people actually look for "outside the wall". If you are just into "getting over the wall" and as a foreigner, you are probably ok with such solutions. If secure and anonymous communication is important to you, maybe don't rely on it. 

2. Shrink the space: VPNs offered to Chinese citizens are shrinking actually. The government works at times slow, but the attempt to prevent citizens from side loading on their mobile devices ( it is still the main approach of most CN citizens to go online ), cracking down more heavily on CN providers of VPNs and growing technical capabilities to identify "malicious" traffic has increasingly tighten the space. Do some "rouge" providers still escape !? Of course. Is Hong Kong still a massive loop hole ?! Of course. 

The outcome is not to build a system that is 100% failsafe. For the time being, they buried that idea ( though I would think that some guys inside the administration would still love to see it ). But the idea is to keep the masses off the global internet. And that has worked ok so far. Sometimes it might have been even too permissible in the eyes of some hardliners, but hey are working on it. It should also be noted, that some of those CN VPNs don't operate in areas like Xinjiang or Tibet ( as those areas run their own iteration of the GFW ).
